query: evidence on diagnostic accuracy and sensitivity of oblique vs lateral views in detecting spondylolysis in adolescents

retrieved_docs.title: Spondylolysis(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: As a consensus rule, a patient's neurologic examination, as well as all laboratory evaluations, including inflammatory markers, are largely unremarkable. There are no universally accepted guidelines for an imaging protocol; however, the initial imaging studies of choice when the condition is suspected are plain radiographs in the posteroanterior, lateral, and oblique views of the lumbosacral region, taken in the standing position. When present, the lesion is most typically visible in the oblique view, which shows the classic "collar on the Scotty dog". It represents the bony defect between the inferior articular surface and the superior articular surface of a single vertebra. As with any stress fracture, plain films may miss the lesion within the first 2 weeks of the injury. Additionally, it has been reported that plain radiographs only have a sensitivity of 33% in detecting spondylolysis. Some authorities advocate axial CT imaging as the test of choice for spondylolysis; however, due to the high prevalence of spondylolysis in the pediatric population and concerns regarding unnecessary radiation exposure, the next advanced imaging modality, typically chosen if plain films are negative, is the MRI. MRI is excellent at detecting bone marrow edema associated with acute pars interarticularis stress injury as well as detailing neural and soft tissue pathologies. The SPECT scan is a suitable alternative to MRI for detecting acute stress fractures and is also useful in determining the acuity of a fracture. It, however, carries the risk of concurrent radiation hazards, which is therefore preferably avoided in the pediatric population. [7] [10] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Pars Interarticularis Injury -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Lumbar disk herniation is relatively uncommon in children and adolescents, with a rate of 3.5%. Children complain about low back or leg pain worsened by bending forward or Valsalva (coughing, sneezing). Scoliosis Idiopathic scoliosis demonstrates a Cobb angle above 10 degrees on a standing coronal radiograph. It affects 1 to 3% of the school-age population. However, it is usually painless in this age group. Infectious Disorders

retrieved_docs.title: Lumbar Spondylolysis and Spondylolisthesis(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of patients with low-back pain typically includes anterior-posterior (AP) and lateral radiographs of the impacted area. Some physicians will obtain radiographs of the entire spine. A key component to radiographic evaluation is obtaining flexion/extension films, which illustrates vertebral segment stability. [18] Serial standing lateral radiographs obtained in the clinic will help track any potential progression of the slip.

retrieved_docs.title: Lumbosacral Spondylolisthesis(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Following the history and examination, the best screening tool is an AP and lateral weight-bearing X-ray of the lumbar spine. [15]  Lumbosacral spondylolisthesis can be best assessed mainly on the lateral view, but occasional coronal deformity should not be missed. In cases where a clinical examination indicates an abnormal sagittal balance of the spinal column, a whole-spine lateral standing X-ray is indicated. In the majority of cases, an isthmic defect is detected on radiographs; however, in cases of doubt, an MRI scan is recommended for further evaluation. Oblique X-rays of the lumbosacral junction, Computerized Tomography (CT) scans, and SPECT scans may also help identify the defect; however, they involve exposure to ionizing radiation.

retrieved_docs.title: Pars Interarticularis Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: SPECT is considered the best diagnostic adjunct when plain radiographs are negative. Several of the abnormalities identified on SPECT proved to represent spinal pathology other than spondylolysis, including infection and osteoid osteoma. Although these modalities may present with increased sensitivity in detecting pars lesions compared with plain film, they are not necessarily highly specific for this disorder. Repetitive stress causes local bone remodeling and abnormal uptake of scintigraphic tracer. SPECT has 10 to 20 times more contrast than planar bone scintigraphy; it is more sensitive than radiography and planar bone scans and improves anatomic localization of skeletal lesions without exposing the patient to additional radiation. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Pars Interarticularis Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Scintigraphy is an excellent screening tool for low back pain in children or adolescents. It has shown high sensitivity for detecting acute injuries and bone stress reactions in the pars. However, some lesions may not display an increased contrast uptake.

retrieved_docs.title: Pars Interarticularis Injury -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of adolescents return to activities after six months of conservative treatment. Skeletally immature children and adolescents with bilateral spondylolysis or spondylolisthesis require standing lateral radiographs to assess the progression of slippage every 6 to 12 months until reaching skeletal maturity. Adolescents with over 50% slip (Grade 3 or 4) warrant orthopedic referral and possible surgical treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Pars Interarticularis Defect(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Given the relatively nonspecific physical exam findings and the known high incidence in particular patient populations, if pars defect is suspected, additional workup ought to include appropriate imaging studies. There is no universally accepted imaging standard for the diagnosis of pars defect. Given the high-risk population includes children and adolescents, close attention to radiation exposure is warranted. With that in mind, initial workup generally begins with AP/lateral/oblique plain radiographs of the lumbar spine. AP/lateral radiograph is sufficient to detect most pars defects, though estimates are that 20% require oblique views to appreciate the lesion. [17] [11] Dynamic plain radiographs may additionally be indicated when there is a concern for instability in the case of spondylolisthesis. On oblique plain radiographs, the common descriptive term for a fracture to the pars interarticularis is as the “collar” on the “Scottie dog.” (figure 1). [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Lumbosacral Spondylolisthesis(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: MRI scans are more sensitive in identifying pars lesions.MRI can also identify stress reactions that occur even before a fracture line develops. [16] In dysplastic cases, a dome-shaped or significantly inclined sacrum can present, as well as trapezoid-shaped L5 and dysplastic facets of S1. Neoplasms and infections are an extremely rare primary cause of spondylolisthesis, but should merit consideration as a differential diagnosis in patients with constitutional symptoms. To assess dynamic instability, flexion and extension views should be obtained. Either 4 mm of translation or 10 degrees of angulation of motion compared to the adjacent motion segment is diagnostic for spondylolisthesis. Grading of the forward slip is classified by the Meyerding classification: Grade I <25% of the width of the vertebra on the lateral view Grade II 25 to 50% Grade III 50 to 75% Grade IV 75 to 100% Grade V >100% (spondyloptosis) Pelvic incidence (PI) has a direct correlation to Meyerding's grade. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Pars Interarticularis Defect(Archived) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Pars defects are surprisingly common in the pediatric/adolescent population who participate in sports and present with low back pain (as high as 50%). With that in mind, approach these patients with an appropriate index of suspicion and work up with imaging. If initial radiographs are negative (AP/lateral/oblique), follow up with MRI or CT. Early identification correlates with a significantly improved outcome.

retrieved_docs.title: Spondylolisthesis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Developmental abnormalities, including spina bifida occulta and congenital scoliosis, are significant in the pediatric population. These disorders may present with deformities resembling spondylolisthesis but lack vertebral displacement. Hip pathology, eg, labral tears or advanced arthritis, should also be considered in cases of radiating pain, as these conditions may simulate the symptoms of lumbar instability or radiculopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Pars Interarticularis Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) offers advantages in terms of visualizing other types of pathology present in the lumbar spine and may potentially detect pars edema secondary to stress in their clinical course. [27] The lack of ionizing radiation with MRI may also make it a desirable modality for studying pars lesions, especially in the female adolescent population. [28] However, it is worth noting that MRI, like CT, does not assess whether a bony lesion is metabolically active and is less sensitive and specific than scintigraphy or SPECT.

retrieved_docs.title: Lumbar Disc Herniation -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: X-rays: Lumbar X-ray films are the first-line imaging test performed in low back pain settings. The standard examination includes three views (AP, lateral, and oblique) to evaluate the overall alignment of the spine, detecting fractures, as well as degenerative or spondylotic changes. Lateral flexion and extension views are useful in assessing spinal instability. Narrowed intervertebral space, traction osteophytes, and compensatory scoliosis on X-ray are findings that usually suggest lumbar disc herniation. If an acute fracture is detected, further investigation with computed tomographic (CT) scan or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is required.

retrieved_docs.title: Nuclear Medicine Pediatric Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Child abuse: Non-accidental injury in babies and children is a challenging clinical conundrum. Diagnosis is crucial, and a high suspicion should always be maintained. Bone scan offers a high sensitivity exam that adds value as an adjunct to the traditional skeletal survey. [121] [122] [123] [124] [125] [126] Bone scans can also be used in additional pathologies such as sports injuries, scoliosis, trauma, back pain after surgery, osteoid osteoma, and Langerhans histiocytosis. Fluoride PET bone scans offer a high resolution and may replace the traditional bone SPECT scan when available. [127]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 983
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as “اعتلال مرتكز العظم” functions as a parent category that includes spondylosis, intervertebral disc disorders, and other back problems. These specific spinal and disc pathologies are therefore understood as manifestations of the broader bone‑centered disease entity, allowing clinicians to classify and address them within a unified diagnostic and therapeutic framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1899
retrieved_docs.content: MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES is a broad classification that encompasses a variety of diagnostic and therapeutic interventions, and within this category the specific procedure صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية is listed as a subordinate (child) procedure, indicating that spinal‑region imaging is considered one of the miscellaneous diagnostic and therapeutic techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 761
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedure is a broad surgical category that falls under the parent grouping “Clinical actions A – G” and comprises a wide array of specific operative techniques. It includes adrenalectomy, partial or complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar or dorsal approaches; cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct and cholangiography; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage or removal of calculi, which may be accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region; exploration of the orbit by a transcranial approach with lesion removal; numerous laminectomy procedures that combine exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord and/or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy or discectomy—ranging from 1‑2 vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis) and thoracic spine to more than two segments in the cervical and thoracic spine; laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and/or excision of a herniated intervertebral disc in single cervical or lumbar interspaces; nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct; and parathyroidectomy or exploration of the parathyroids, including variants with mediastinal exploration performed via cervical, sternal‑split or transthoracic approaches. Some of these methods are listed as possibly included within the exploration category, reflecting flexibility in how they may be combined or selected. Together, these procedures illustrate the diverse yet interrelated nature of exploratory surgeries used for diagnostic assessment and therapeutic intervention across multiple organ systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2602
retrieved_docs.content: MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES is a broad procedural category that includes various imaging modalities. Computerized axial tomography (CT) scan is classified as a child of this category, indicating its role as a diagnostic imaging technique used for cross‑sectional visualization of internal structures. The procedure described in Arabic as صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية (spinal cord imaging of the spinal region) is also listed as a child of MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES, highlighting its inclusion as another specialized diagnostic imaging method. Together, these relationships illustrate that both CT scanning and spinal cord imaging are organized under the same overarching procedural group, emphasizing their shared purpose in providing diagnostic information for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1713
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to examine, diagnose, or treat pathology within various anatomical regions. Specific operations that are classified as methods of exploration include partial or complete adrenalectomy—or adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy—performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and may also involve excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. In hepatobiliary surgery, cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, whether accompanied by cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty, is regarded as an exploration method. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy performed for investigative purposes, as well as suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing cranial nerves. Orthopedic exploration of the spinal canal is achieved through laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; this technique is applied to one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), and sacral regions, and may be listed as a possibly included method in broader procedural codes. Additional exploration procedures encompass nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, which can be performed alone or with mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, including specific transthoracic mediastinal exploration. Collectively, these diverse surgical methods illustrate how exploration procedures are tailored to the target organ system and clinical objective, serving both diagnostic and therapeutic roles in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2449
retrieved_docs.content: Spondylopathies/spondyloarthropathy (including infective) is a classification that encompasses spondylosis, intervertebral disc disorders, and other back problems, grouping these diverse spinal conditions under a common diagnostic category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1106
retrieved_docs.content: Spondylosis, intervertebral disc disorders, and other back problems are all considered subcategories of اضطراب العمود الفقري (spinal disorder), indicating that these specific back conditions fall under the broader classification of spinal disorder and should be approached within that overarching diagnostic and therapeutic framework.

Spinal exploration includes cervical, thoracic, lumbar, and sacral laminectomy/laminotomy techniques that provide exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; single‑segment procedures involve one to two vertebral levels, whereas multi‑segment procedures involve more than two levels. Excision is the operative method underlying many of these exploration procedures and serves as the parent concept for “Clinical actions A‑G,” which in turn parents the broader Exploration procedure class. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2366
retrieved_docs.content: Plain X-ray techniques encompass tomografie as a subordinate imaging method, indicating that tomografie is classified as a child technique within the broader category of plain X-ray imaging. This relationship highlights that tomografie derives from or is considered a specialized extension of standard plain X-ray procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 534
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized axial tomography (CT) scan is classified under the broader category MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES, which itself is a subcategory of Ophthalmologic and otologic diagnosis and treatment. Within this hierarchy, Computerized axial tomography (CT) scan includes the specific child procedure التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر (computed tomography of the chest). MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES also has the child procedure صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية (spinal cord imaging of the vertebral region). The procedure Other chest x-ray is positioned as a subordinate to التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر, indicating that it is a related imaging modality within the chest CT framework. This structured organization clarifies how various imaging studies—CT scanning, chest‑specific CT, spinal imaging, and related chest radiography—are nested within broader diagnostic and therapeutic procedure categories, supporting clinicians in navigating and selecting appropriate imaging modalities for patient care.

Neurosurgical exploration includes both craniectomy and craniotomy performed for investigative purposes, with infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial variants identified as specific methods. Suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploration or decompression of cranial nerves represents another dedicated technique, while transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal extends the scope of exploratory surgery to the orbit. Spinal exploration is represented by laminectomy procedures that provide access to and decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without accompanying facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. These laminectomies are detailed for one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), sacral, and thoracic regions, and for more than two segments in the cervical and thoracic spine, each described as a method that may be included in an overall exploration procedure. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2056
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized axial tomography (CT) scan and contrast arteriogram of femoral and lower extremity arteries are both categorized under the broader group labeled MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES, indicating that they are considered part of a diverse set of imaging and interventional techniques used for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes. Within this same category, the procedure described in Arabic as صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية (spinal cord imaging of the spinal region) is identified as a child procedure, further illustrating that MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES encompasses a range of specialized studies, from CT scanning and vascular contrast arteriography to spinal cord imaging, all of which contribute to comprehensive patient assessment and care.

In addition to its procedural scope, Excision is identified as the answer to the primary diagnostic procedure for colorectal cancer within certain clinical coding frameworks, underscoring its relevance in oncologic evaluation. This integrated view positions Excision as a versatile operative approach encompassing adrenal, biliary, cranial, and facial surgeries, while delineating clear boundaries that separate it from specific spinal laminectomy techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2115
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures encompass a broad range of operative techniques in which surgeons directly inspect, access, or remove pathology from specific anatomic regions. The category includes adrenal gland surgery such as adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Biliary exploration is represented by several cholecystectomy variants that incorporate common duct exploration, cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty, each designed to evaluate and treat ductal stones or strictures. Cranial exploration is covered by both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy procedures, which allow direct visualization of intracranial lesions. Orbital pathology can be addressed through a transcranial approach to explore the orbit and excise lesions. Spinal exploration procedures include laminectomies—cervical, sacral, lumbar, thoracic, or multilevel—performed without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy to decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina in conditions such as spinal stenosis; these may be listed as core methods or as possibly included techniques depending on the extent of vertebral involvement. A related laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) technique provides nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated disc material at a single lumbar level, often as a re‑exploration. Parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach exemplifies neck and mediastinal access for hyperparathyroidism. All of these specific methods fall under the broader “Exploration procedure” classification, which itself is organized under the parent category “Clinical actions A‑G.” This hierarchical structure clarifies how diverse surgical explorations are grouped for clinical documentation, coding, and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2345
retrieved_docs.content: Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus is classified as a subtype within the broader category described as Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, indicating that this fetal presentation falls under a parent classification of transverse or oblique presentations without a defined episode of care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1573
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures encompass a wide range of surgical techniques used to investigate, diagnose, or treat pathology in diverse anatomic regions. The category includes adrenal gland operations such as the partial or complete adrenalectomy or adrenal exploration performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, which may also involve biopsy and, when indicated, excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Biliary exploration is represented by cholecystectomy with common duct investigation, either through cholangiography or choledochoenterostomy, and can be performed laparoscopically. Cranial exploration procedures comprise exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the supratentorial compartment and suboccipital craniectomy aimed at decompression of cranial nerves, as well as transcranial orbital exploration for lesion removal. Spinal exploration is addressed by multilevel laminectomies of the cervical, thoracic, or lumbar spine that allow decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without additional facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy, and by lumbar laminotomies (hemilaminectomies) that include nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc fragment excision in a single‑level re‑exploration. Parathyroid exploration procedures include parathyroidectomy with mediastinal access achieved through cervical, sternal‑split, or transthoracic approaches. All of these specific methods are organized under the broader parent category “Clinical actions A‑G,” which groups related exploratory surgical interventions for systematic clinical documentation and management.
